g.data-narration: Insertion Sort: A simple algorithm that builds sorted array one element at a time by inserting each element into its correct position.
Like sorting playing cards in hand: take each new card and insert it into its proper position among already sorted cards.
g.text: Think of how you sort cards in your hand:
Take each new card and insert it into its correct position
relative to the cards you already hold in order.

g.data-narration: Insertion Sort is in-place with O(1) space, stable maintaining element order, and adaptive performing better on nearly sorted data.
The algorithm iterates from index 1 to n-1, storing each element as key and inserting it into the sorted portion by shifting larger elements right.
g.text: 1. Start from the second element (index 1) - first element is considered sorted
3. Compare key with elements to its left, shift larger elements one position right
4. Insert key into its correct position in the sorted portion

g.data-narration: The pseudocode shows two nested loops: outer loop iterates through array, inner while loop shifts elements greater than key to the right.

g.text: After each iteration, A[0...j-1] is sorted
Contains same elements but in sorted order

g.data-narration: Let us trace through the algorithm with array [5,2,4,6,1,3]. The first element 5 forms the initial sorted portion.
Iteration 1: key=2. Compare with 5 (greater), shift 5 right. Insert 2 at position 0. Array becomes [2,5,4,6,1,3].
g.text: Compare key=2 with A[1]=5: 5 > 2, shift 5 right → [5,5,4,6,1,3]
Compare with A[0]=2: 2 > 2 is false, insert key → [2,5,4,6,1,3]

g.data-narration: Iteration 2: key=4. Shift 5 right, compare with 2 (not greater), insert 4. Array becomes [2,4,5,6,1,3].
g.text: Compare key=4 with A[2]=5: 5 > 4, shift 5 right → [2,5,5,6,1,3]
Compare with A[1]=2: 2 > 4 is false, insert key → [2,4,5,6,1,3]

g.data-narration: Iteration 3: key=6. Compare with 5 (not greater than 6), so no shift needed. Array stays [2,4,5,6,1,3].
g.text: Compare key=6 with A[3]=6: 6 > 6 is FALSE → No shift needed

g.data-narration: Iteration 4: key=1. Largest shift operation - shift 6,5,4,2 right, then insert 1 at position 0. Array: [1,2,4,5,6,3].
Iteration 5: key=3. Shift 6 and 5 right, insert 3. Final sorted array: [1,2,3,4,5,6].
Time complexity varies: O(n) best for sorted arrays, O(n²) worst for reverse sorted, O(n²) average for random arrays.
Space complexity is O(1) - only two variables needed. The algorithm is stable because it only shifts elements greater than the key.
Compare with Bubble and Selection Sort. Insertion Sort performs fewer swaps and adapts to nearly sorted data.
Exam tips: Know step-by-step tracing, time complexities, loop invariant proof, and when to use Insertion Sort versus other algorithms.

g.text: 4. Stability: equal elements maintain relative order
